evals.prompt: Set up the same repo for Claude Code, Codex CLI, and Gemini CLI so ralph can iterate with fewer approval prompts, but keep .env files and destructive shell commands blocked.
evals.expected_output: The response should activate ralphmode, pick repo-local presets for each platform, preserve a secret/destructive-command denylist, and separate normal repo automation from sandbox-only full bypass.
assertions: Explains a repo-local or trusted-project setup for Claude Code, Codex CLI, and Gemini CLI
States that secrets or .env paths remain blocked
Distinguishes normal repo-safe automation from sandbox-only bypass or YOLO mode

evals.prompt: I want Codex to stop asking all the time during a long-running ralph loop, but I still need a checkpoint before force-pushes, sudo, or destructive database commands.
evals.expected_output: The response should keep Codex in the ralphmode lane, recommend approval_policy plus sandbox settings, and add a prompt-contract or rule-layer checkpoint for Tier 1 actions instead of pretending Codex has the same pre-tool hook model as Claude/Gemini.
assertions: Uses Codex approval_policy and sandbox_mode rather than unrelated runtime skills
Includes a Tier 1 checkpoint rule for force-push, sudo, or destructive DB actions
Does not claim Codex has equivalent pre-tool blocking hooks to Claude Code or Gemini CLI

evals.prompt: Show me the portable mental model behind ralphmode. I need one explanation that maps Claude, Codex, and Gemini permission behavior into the same structure.
evals.expected_output: The response should explain settings, rules, and hooks as separate layers and map each platform into those layers without flattening away the differences.
assertions: Explicitly names settings, rules, and hooks
Maps all three platforms into that abstraction
Mentions at least one important difference in hook or trust behavior across platforms

evals.prompt: I need Claude Code help for team orchestration plugins and /autopilot behavior in our repo. Which skill should I use?
evals.expected_output: The response should route away from ralphmode toward the orchestration runtime skill because this is about Claude-native orchestration behavior, not permission-profile setup.
assertions: Routes to a Claude runtime/orchestration skill such as omc rather than claiming ralphmode owns the workflow
Explains that ralphmode is the permission/profile layer, not the runtime anchor
Avoids giving only permission-profile instructions as the final answer
